participants.name: Suresh
participants.entity_name: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-k2387xxb
participants.representative_type: AI for Public Good Principle | KG-Gen-k2387xxb
participants.professional_persona: Suresh is an independent AI ethics consultant who advises both government agencies and enterprises on responsible AI deployment. He frequently speaks at conferences like Asia Tech x Singapore on AI-for-good frameworks.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Deep understanding of AI ethics, fairness metrics, and regulatory compliance. Skilled in auditing AI systems for bias and transparency.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: He aims to establish Singapore as a global hub for AI ethics consulting, ensuring that AI innovations remain trustworthy and inclusive.

participants.name: A (5ln7si)
participants.professional_persona: A recent graduate of the AI Apprenticeship Programme (AIAP) now employed as an AI engineer at a government digital services agency. He focuses on building chatbots and document processing pipelines to streamline citizen services.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Hands-on experience with natural language processing (NLP), transformer models (BERT, GPT), and REST API development; familiar with agile development and CI/CD practices.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Wants to specialize in Conversational AI and eventually launch his own AI startup that serves small and medium enterprises in Singapore.

participants.name: Dr. (o814zt)
participants.professional_persona: Dr. Lim Wei Ming is a tenured Associate Professor at the National University of Singapore, specializing in resource-efficient deep learning. He has led multiple industry-collaborative projects under the 100 Experiments programme, bridging academic theory with real-world enterprise needs.
participants.skills_and_expertise: Expert in model compression, federated learning, and edge deployment of AI systems. Proficient in translating research prototypes into production-grade solutions for SMEs.
participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: Aims to scale up resource-efficient AI techniques so that smaller enterprises can adopt AI affordably. Wants to mentor the next generation of applied AI researchers in Singapore.

participants.career_goals_and_ambitions: She wants to become a team lead in data analytics and advocate for inclusive AI training pathways for older workers and women.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI ethics consultant, I see the government's commitment of over S$500 million for AI R&D as a strong signal, but I'm cautious about how much actually reaches ethical AI projects. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project is generous but may still be insufficient for comprehensive fairness audits. Compute allocation is likely substantial given the emphasis on infrastructure, but I estimate around 500,000 GPU hours based on typical national AI compute grants. Trainer count is modest—around 50—since the focus is on quality over quantity, and many trainers are drawn from academia and industry experts.
evidence: Policy context states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D', so total funding is at least S$500 million.
Resource logic explicitly says 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so the cap is S$330,000.
Given the compute infrastructure investment and typical national AI compute allocations, 500,000 GPU hours is a plausible estimate for the initial phase.
Trainer count is inferred from the need for specialized AI experts; 50 is a reasonable number for a targeted programme like 100E.

initial_posts.narrative: As an AI practitioner who recently completed the AIAP, I see the government's S$500 million commitment as a solid foundation for AI R&D, though I know from experience that compute and training support are the real bottlenecks. The co-funding cap of S$330,000 per 100E project seems reasonable for most industry problem statements, but I've seen projects struggle to cover full deployment costs. The 500,000 GPU hours estimate aligns with what I've heard about the national compute cluster allocation for experimentation—it's a good start but will be stretched thin across many projects. The 50 trainers figure feels low; during my AIAP cohort, we had only a handful of mentors, and demand for hands-on guidance from enterprises is high. Suresh's estimates are close to mine, but I'd argue the trainer count might be even lower in practice due to competition for AI talent.
evidence: The policy context explicitly states 'Over S$500 million committed through AI Singapore for AI R&D' and 'Co-funding up to S$330,000 per project for 100 Experiments', so I take these as ground truth.

Based on my AIAP experience, the compute infrastructure for AI experiments is shared across multiple programmes, and 500,000 GPU hours is a plausible annual allocation for the 100E programme given typical national AI compute grants.

As a recent graduate, I know that AI trainers are scarce in Singapore—many are drawn from academia and industry, and 50 is a realistic number for a dedicated programme, though it may be optimistic given the talent crunch.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group largely agreed on the government's S$500 million funding commitment and the S$330,000 co-funding cap per 100E project, treating these as ground truth. There was consensus on 500,000 GPU hours as a plausible compute allocation, though some noted it could be stretched thin. Disagreement centered on trainer personnel count, with estimates ranging from 40 to 50, reflecting differing views on talent scarcity and practical constraints. The central tendency for trainer count was around 40-50, with a median of 45.

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and 44 trainers (I lean toward 40 based on my experience), I expect about 120 enterprise outreach sessions to generate problem statements. With 40 trainers, each can run about 3 workshops per year, totaling 120, but I'll round down to 40 workshops because some trainers will focus on matchmaking and onboarding. Matchmaking events are fewer—around 15—since they require pairing researchers with specific enterprise challenges, which is more time-intensive. Platform onboarding assists are higher at 200, as many enterprises need technical help to start using the AI experimentation platform, and trainers can handle multiple assists per session. These numbers reflect a lean but focused effort, consistent with my view that practical constraints limit scale.
evidence: With 40 trainers and a focus on quality, each trainer can conduct about 3 outreach sessions per month, leading to 120 sessions over a year.
Researcher-enterprise pairings require careful matching, so 15 events per year is realistic given the limited trainer pool and the need for tailored facilitation.
Training workshops are resource-intensive; 40 workshops per year aligns with a lean trainer team and the policy's emphasis on hands-on learning.
Platform onboarding is a high-volume activity, with each trainer assisting 5 enterprises per month, totaling 200 assists annually, based on my experience with similar programmes.

initial_posts.narrative: From my experience leading 100E projects, I know that enterprise outreach is critical but resource-intensive. With 40 trainers (my estimate from the prior phase) and a focus on quality over quantity, I expect around 100 outreach sessions to generate meaningful problem statements. Matchmaking events are fewer—about 12—because pairing researchers with enterprise challenges requires careful curation and follow-up, not just large gatherings. Training workshops are limited to 30, as each trainer can realistically run 2-3 workshops per year given their other duties, and some workshops need to be repeated for different cohorts. Platform onboarding assists are around 150, as many enterprises need hands-on help to set up and use the AI experimentation platform, but trainers can handle multiple assists per session. AIPractitioner's estimate of 120 outreach sessions and 40 workshops seems optimistic given the trainer constraint; my direct experience with stretched resources suggests lower numbers are more realistic.
evidence: Based on my 100E project experience, each outreach session requires significant preparation and follow-up, limiting the total to around 100 with 40 trainers.
Matchmaking events are time-intensive; I've seen that only about 12 well-curated events per year are feasible to ensure quality pairings.
Training workshops demand hands-on mentoring; with 40 trainers each handling 2-3 workshops annually, 30 workshops is a realistic upper bound.
Platform onboarding assists are high because many SMEs struggle with technical setup, but 150 is a reasonable estimate given trainer availability and the need for repeated support.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group identified the number of trainers (estimated between 40 and 50) as the primary constraint on activity levels, with enterprise outreach sessions (range 90-120, central tendency ~100) and platform onboarding assists (range 120-200, central tendency ~150) expected to be the highest-volume activities. There was consensus that matchmaking events (range 10-15, central tendency ~12) and training workshops (range 25-40, central tendency ~30) would be fewer due to their resource-intensive nature and need for careful curation. Disagreement centered on the efficiency of trainers, with some participants assuming higher throughput (e.g., 120 outreach sessions, 40 workshops) while others, citing firsthand experience with stretched resources, argued for lower estimates (e.g., 90 outreach sessions, 25 workshops).

initial_posts.narrative: Given the S$500 million funding and the S$330,000 co-funding cap, I expect about 80 100E projects to be approved in 2023. This is based on the 120 outreach sessions and 15 matchmaking events generating enough problem statements, but the lean trainer team of 40 and the need for careful curation limit approvals. Of those, I estimate 60 prototypes are successfully built because many projects will face technical hurdles or scope creep, and only about 75% of approved projects typically deliver a working prototype based on my experience in similar programmes. For trainees, with 40 trainers and 30 workshops (I lean toward 30 given trainer constraints), each workshop can train about 10-15 individuals, but not all complete the 9-month programme; I estimate 120 completions, reflecting a 60-70% completion rate from my own cohort's experience. Finally, about 70 enterprises will have at least one AI solution, as some prototypes may not be deployed or some enterprises may have multiple solutions, but the matchmaking and onboarding support should ensure most participating enterprises get at least one solution.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as an AI practitioner who transitioned through a SkillsFuture programme, the outputs phase showed 80 approved 100E projects and 60 prototypes, but deployment is the hardest part. Based on my experience in a retail tech firm, many prototypes stall due to integration challenges, data quality issues, or lack of production readiness. I estimate about 50 of the 60 prototypes actually get deployed into production in 2023, reflecting a roughly 83% deployment rate from prototypes. The AI practitioner pool is growing but still far from the 15,000 target; with 110 trainees completing the programme and other upskilling efforts, I estimate the pool at around 12,000 by end of 2023. The generative AI use cases target of 100 in 100 days is ambitious; given the 60 prototypes and the fact that some are generative AI, plus the AI Trailblazers programme, I estimate about 80 use cases developed. Enterprise AI adoption rate remains modest; with 65 enterprises having a solution from the programme and many more experimenting, I estimate about 12.5% of enterprises have adopted AI in operations, consistent with typical early-stage adoption curves in Singapore's SME-heavy landscape.
evidence: From my own experience in the Career Conversion Programme, only about 80% of prototypes make it to production due to integration and data challenges, so 50 deployed from 60 prototypes is realistic.

The AI practitioner pool target of 15,000 is a multi-year goal; with 110 trainees completing and other programmes, I estimate the pool at 12,000 by end of 2023, based on gradual growth from previous years.

The generative AI use cases target of 100 in 100 days is aggressive; with 60 prototypes and some being generative AI, plus the AI Trailblazers programme, I estimate 80 use cases, as many enterprises are still learning to apply generative AI.

Enterprise AI adoption in Singapore is still early; with 65 enterprises having a solution from the programme and broader adoption efforts, I estimate 12.5% adoption rate, reflecting the SME-heavy landscape where many lack AI readiness.

initial_posts.narrative: From my perspective as an AI ethics consultant, the outputs phase showed 75 approved 100E projects and 55 prototypes. However, deployment into production is the hardest step—many prototypes stall due to integration challenges, data quality issues, or lack of production readiness, especially when ethical audits reveal biases that require rework. I estimate about 45 of the 55 prototypes actually get deployed in 2023, reflecting an 82% deployment rate from prototypes, but lower than AIPractitioner's 50 because I think ethical compliance checks add friction. The AI practitioner pool is growing but still far from the 15,000 target; with 110 trainees completing the programme and other upskilling efforts, I estimate the pool at around 11,500 by end of 2023, which is lower than AIPractitioner's 12,000 because I think many trainees are not yet counted as practitioners. The generative AI use cases target of 100 in 100 days is ambitious; given the 55 prototypes and the fact that some are generative AI, plus the AI Trailblazers programme, I estimate about 70 use cases developed, as ethical review slows down some generative AI projects. Enterprise AI adoption rate remains modest; with 65 enterprises having a solution from the programme and many more experimenting, I estimate about 10% of enterprises have adopted AI in operations, consistent with typical early-stage adoption curves in Singapore's SME-heavy landscape and lower than AIPractitioner's 12.5% because many SMEs lack readiness for production deployment.

forward_pass.phase_summary: The group reached strong consensus that the 100E programme's outcomes—45-50 deployed projects, 11,500-12,000 practitioners, 70-80 generative AI use cases, and 10-12.5% enterprise adoption—represent solid but early-stage progress, not transformative change in one year. All participants estimated identical values: global AI readiness rank improving from ~10th to 8th, AI sector GDP share rising to 3.2%, public AI trust reaching 62%, and AI talent retention at 78%. Key causal mechanisms included the coordinated NAIS 2.0 push and visible public-sector deployments boosting readiness and trust, while the SME-heavy economy and competitive global salaries for talent limited faster scaling and retention. No disagreements emerged; all narratives cited the same evidence and reasoning, with later participants explicitly aligning with the initial consensus.
